explicit_text: I first learned about the September 11 attacks on the morning of the event while walking to the G Street office. I heard on a radio about a plane crashing, and soon found out two planes had hit the World Trade Center. Later, I saw the Pentagon burning from the office, which was a shocking and terrifying sight. The day was filled with confusion, rumors, and gridlocked traffic as we tried to get out of the city. The attacks deeply affected our work environment and led to immediate security and evacuation changes.

explicit_text: As part of the National Park Service, I was responsible for employee safety and health during the September 11 attacks. We were in the middle of moving administrative staff to the G Street office when the attacks happened, which complicated our security concerns. The Park Service stepped up to help organize employees during evacuations and worked closely with leadership like Fran Mainella and Rick Gale. We also took on the responsibility of securing national icons like the Washington Monument and Mount Rushmore in the aftermath. It was a challenging time, especially as our focus shifted from security to also include employee safety during the anthrax attacks.

explicit_text: Before the September 11 attacks, the National Park Service was moving a third of its administrative staff to the G Street office, which was located near the World Bank headquarters. Security at the G Street building was poor, with no checks at the garage or entrance, which was a big concern given the anticipated protests around that time. Because of these concerns, we moved staff back to the Main Interior Building just a week before the attacks. After security improvements were promised, employees moved back to G Street shortly before September 11. The building's location and security challenges made it a focal point in our response planning.

explicit_text: After evacuating the G Street office on September 11, we tried to leave the city by car and encountered massive traffic jams, especially around the Roosevelt Bridge. It was a surreal experience seeing so many people trying to escape the city on foot and by car. The Roosevelt Bridge became a symbol of the chaos and confusion that day.

explicit_text: The Main Interior Building served as a fallback location for National Park Service employees when we moved them back from the insecure G Street office just before September 11. On the morning of the attacks, I had difficulty getting back into the building because it was locked down tightly for security. The building became a central place for meetings and coordinating the response, including evacuations to the basement under Deputy Secretary Steve Griles' direction. Since then, we've developed detailed evacuation plans and practiced them regularly in the Main Interior Building.

explicit_text: Steve Griles was the Deputy Secretary who played a key role during the immediate aftermath of the September 11 attacks. He directed employees in the Main Interior Building to evacuate to the basement when there was a potential threat of another plane coming from Canada. He also used a megaphone to communicate with staff during the evacuation because the building's communication system was inadequate. His leadership was important in managing the crisis within the Department of the Interior.
